title: Who Seeks Cancer Information? Predictors in a Nationally Representative U.S. Sample.
text:
Methods

Data Source
This study used data from the Health Information National Trends Survey, Cycle 7 (HINTS 7), which is a nationally representative, cross-sectional survey of U.S. adults aged 18. The survey was conducted by the National Cancer Institute (National Cancer Institute, 2024). Data were collected between March and September 2024 using both mail and web-based surveys.The final sample consisted of 7,278 respondents. HINTS 7 is representative and uses a complex sampling design with with oversampling of minority populations to ensure adequate representation across the demographic groups.


Measures
The outcome variable of interest for this project was cancer information seeking, which was measured by a single item asking whether the respondent had sought information about cancer in the past year (yes/no). We had two key predictors which were information environment variables: exposure to misleading health information on social media (five categories: none, a little, some, a lot, and does not use social media) and frequency of encountering conflicting health recommendations (never, rarely, often, very often). Attitudinal predictors included our analysis were: trust in doctors for cancer information (not at all, a little, some, a lot) and cancer fatalism, measured by agreement with the statement that cancer prevention is not possible (strongly agree to strongly disagree). Demographic covariates included race/ethnicity, education, household income, age group, birth sex, personal cancer history, and health insurance status. Full details on variable coding are available on the About the Data page.


Statistical Analysis
Our analyses accounted for the complex sampling design of HINTS 7. Survey-weighted proportions were calculated to describe the prevalence of cancer information seeking across demographic groups. A survey-weighted logistic regression model was then fit to identify independent predictors of cancer information seeking. The results as reported as adjusted odds ratios with with 95% confidence intervals. All analyses were conducted in R.

title: Who Seeks Cancer Information? Predictors in a Nationally Representative U.S. Sample.
text:
Results

Prevalence of Cancer Information Seeking
Overall, approximately half 51% of U.S. adults reported seeking cancer information in the past year. Table 1 shows the weighted prevalence of cancer information seeking across demographic and attitudinal groups. Cancer information seeking increased with education level among adults with less than high school education to college graduates. There were also racial disparities with Non-Hispanic Whites having the highest information seeking rates compared to Hispanic and Non-Hispanic Black and Non-Hispanic Asian adults.








Figure 1: Weighted prevalence of information seeking by groups.




Figure 1. Weighted prevalence of cancer information seeking by demographic group




Predictors of Cancer Information Seeking
Table 2 presents adjusted odds ratios from the survey-weighted logistic regression model. Figure 2 displays these results visually as a forest plot, with blue points indicating statistically significant predictors (p < 0.05).
The strongest predictor of cancer information seeking was a personal history of cancer. Adults who had a cancer diagnosis had over four times the odds of seeking cancer information compared to those without (OR = 4.02, 95% CI: 2.96–5.45, p < 0.001). Frequently encountering conflicting health recommendations was also strongly associated with seeking cancer information.Compared to adults who never encountered conflicting recommendations, those who encountered them rarely had 3.78 times the odds of seeking information (OR = 3.78, 95% CI: 2.09–6.84, p < 0.001), those who encountered them often had 4.82 times the odds (OR = 4.82, 95% CI: 2.67–8.68, p < 0.001), and those who encountered them very often had 5.59 times the odds (OR = 5.59, 95% CI: 2.90–10.80, p < 0.001).
Higher education was associated with greater seeking. College graduates had approximately 2.79 times the odds of seeking cancer information compared to those with less than a high school education (OR = 2.79, 95% CI: 1.48–5.25, p = 0.002). Higher income was also associated with information seeking. Adults earning $75,000–$99,999 (OR = 1.48, 95% CI: 1.05–2.09, p = 0.027) and $100,000 or more (OR = 1.44, 95% CI: 1.07–1.94, p = 0.017) had significantly higher odds than those earning less than $35,000. Adults aged 35–49 had higher odds of seeking cancer information than those aged 18–34 (OR = 1.39, 95% CI: 1.03–1.88, p = 0.032). Compared to females, males had significantly lower odds of seeking information (OR = 0.76, 95% CI: 0.62–0.93, p = 0.007). Non-Hispanic Black adults had significantly lower odds of seeking compared to Non-Hispanic White adults (OR = 0.72, 95% CI: 0.54–0.95, p = 0.021), though differences for other racial and ethnic groups were not statistically significant after adjustment.
Notably, exposure to misleading health information on social media was positively associated with seeking cancer information. Those exposed to “a lot” of misleading information had higher odds of seeking than those exposed to none. However this association was not statistically significant. Trust in doctors for cancer information was also not significantly associated with seeking after adjusting for other factors.






Table 2. Survey-weighted logistic regression predicting cancer information seeking, HINTS 7 (2024)

Abbreviations: CI = Confidence Interval, OR = Odds Ratio










A forest-plot with adjusted odds ratios for predictors of information seeking




Figure 2. Adjusted odds ratios for predictors of cancer information seeking, HINTS 7 (2024)




Among Seekers: Was the Information Easy to Find?
As shown in figure 3, 59.1% of information seekers expressed concern about the quality of the information they found, making it the most commonly reported difficulty. Additionally, 41.8% found the information too hard to understand, 41.0% reported that it took a lot of effort to find cancer information, and 38.1% felt frustrated during their search. These findings suggest that even among adults who actively seek cancer information, the experience is often difficult and the quality of available information is a major concern.




Figure 3. Experiences among adults who sought cancer information, HINTS 7 (2024)




The Misinformation Paradox
One of the interesting finding from this analysis was the relationship between exposure to misleading health information on social media and cancer information seeking. Although one might expect misleading information to discourage engagement, higher exposure to misinformation was instead associated with greater cancer information seeking. Figure 4 illustrates this pattern. There was a clear increase in cancer information seeking with greater exposure to misleading health information, rising from 30.1% among those with no exposure to 58.4% among those with high exposure. Adults who did not use social media had an intermediate seeking rate of 37.7%. While this association was not statistically significant after adjusting for other factors in the regression model.




Figure 4. Cancer information seeking by level of misleading health information exposure, HINTS 7 (2024)

title: Who Seeks Cancer Information? Predictors in a Nationally Representative U.S. Sample.
text:
Conclusion and Summary
This study used data from the 2024 Health Information National Trends Survey (HINTS 7) to examine what predicts cancer information seeking among U.S. adults. Overall, approximately half of U.S. adults (51.1%) reported seeking cancer information in the past year, though this varied substantially across demographic and attitudinal groups.
The strongest predictor of cancer information seeking was a personal history of cancer, with survivors having over four times the odds of seeking information. This underscores the critical role of health information access in cancer survivorship and ongoing care. Encountering conflicting health recommendations was also strongly associated with information seeking, suggesting that people actively search for information when they are uncertain.
Education and income disparities in cancer information seeking were evident even after adjusting for other factors in the model. Adults with less education and lower incomes were significantly less likely to seek cancer information, which might be reflective of structural barriers in health information access. One of the most intriguing findings was a “misinformation paradox,” where adults exposed to higher levels of misleading health information on social media were more likely to seek cancer information. This pattern suggests that exposure to misinformation may act as a motivator rather than a barrier, potentially prompting individuals to verify the information they encounter.

Limitations
This study has several limitations.

HINTS 7 is a cross-sectional survey which makes it difficult to establish causal relationships.
All measures were self-reported and subject to recall and social desirability bias.
The race/ethnicity variable had the highest amount of missing values (11.5%), which may affect the precision of estimates for racial subgroups.
The survey captures whether cancer information was sought but does not include details on the quality or source of information found.



Future Directions
Future research could explore the misinformation paradox to understand whether early misinformation exposure leads to sustained information seeking over time. There is a need for interventions targeting low-education and low-income adults to help reduce disparities in cancer information access.

title: About the Data
text: The data used in this analysis was obtained from the Health Information National Trends Survey, Cycle 7 (HINTS 7), which was administered by the National Cancer Institute (NCI). HINTS is a nationally representative, cross-sectional survey of U.S. adults aged 18 and older. The survey was conducted between March and September 2024 using a mail and web-based design.This is a publicly available dataset that can be accessed at hints.cancer.gov.

title: About the Data
text: The data used in this analysis was obtained from the Health Information National Trends Survey, Cycle 7 (HINTS 7), which was administered by the National Cancer Institute (NCI). HINTS is a nationally representative, cross-sectional survey of U.S. adults aged 18 and older. The survey was conducted between March and September 2024 using a mail and web-based design.This is a publicly available dataset that can be accessed at hints.cancer.gov.

title: About the Data
text:
Loading the data


Code
load(here("data", "hints7_public.rda"))
df_raw <- public

nrow(df_raw)
ncol(df_raw)

title: About the Data
text:
Variables Used
The following variables were selected for this analysis.


Code
tibble(
  Variable = c(
    "SeekCancerInfo",
    "MisleadingHealthInfo",
    "HealthRecsConflict",
    "CancerTrustDoctor",
    "PreventNotPossible",
    "RaceEthn5",
    "EducA",
    "IncomeRanges_IMP",
    "AgeGrpB",
    "BirthSex",
    "EverHadCancer",
    "HealthInsurance2"
  ),
  Description = c(
    "Whether the respondent sought cancer information in the past year",
    "How much misleading health information seen on social media",
    "How often health recommendations seem to conflict with each other",
    "Level of trust in doctors as a source of cancer information",
    "Belief that cancer prevention is not possible (fatalism)",
    "Race and ethnicity (5 categories)",
    "Highest level of education completed",
    "Annual household income (imputed for missing values)",
    "Age group",
    "Birth sex",
    "Whether the respondent has ever been diagnosed with cancer",
    "Whether the respondent has health insurance"
  ),
  Role = c(
    "Outcome",
    "Key predictor",
    "Key predictor",
    "Key predictor",
    "Key predictor",
    "Demographic",
    "Demographic",
    "Demographic",
    "Demographic",
    "Demographic",
    "Covariate",
    "Covariate"
  ),
  Type = c(
    "Binary (Yes/No)",
    "Ordinal — 4 levels + no social media",
    "Ordinal — Never / Rarely / Often / Very Often",
    "Ordinal — Not at all / A little / Some / A lot",
    "Ordinal — Strongly agree to Strongly disagree",
    "Categorical",
    "Ordinal — 4 levels",
    "Ordinal — collapsed to 4 levels",
    "Ordinal — 5 groups",
    "Binary (Female/Male)",
    "Binary (Yes/No)",
    "Binary (Yes/No)"
  )
) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Variables used in the analysis") |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_fill(color = "#E6F1FB"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = Role == "Outcome")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_fill(color = "#E1F5EE"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = Role == "Key predictor")
  ) |>
  cols_width(
    Description ~ px(280),
    Variable ~ px(160),
    Role ~ px(110),
    Type ~ px(200)
  )

title: About the Data
text:
Data Cleaning

Outcome variable — Cancer information seeking
The outcome variable SeekCancerInfo was originally coded as a character variable with values “Yes” and “No”. This was recoded into a binary numeric variable (seek_cancer_info) where Yes = 1 and No = 0 for logistic regression.


Key predictor variables

Information Environment Variables
Two variables capturing the information environment were converted into ordered factors:

MisleadingHealthInfo : how much misleading health information the respondent encountered on social media was recoded into misleading_info with five ordered levels: None, A little, Some, A lot, and I do not use social media. The “no social media” category was kept as a separate level rather than treated as missing, because it represents a meaningfully different group of adults who are not exposed to social media health content at all.
HealthRecsConflict: how often the respondent encounters conflicting health recommendations was recoded into recs_conflict with four ordered levels: Never, Rarely, Often, and Very Often.



Attitudinal Variables
Two attitudinal variables were converted into ordered factors:

CancerTrustDoctor: trust in doctors for cancer information was recoded into trust_doctor with four levels ordered from least to most trust: Not at all, A little, Some, A lot.
PreventNotPossible: agreement with the statement that cancer prevention is not possible was recoded into fatalism with four levels ordered from most to least fatalistic: Strongly agree, Somewhat agree, Somewhat disagree, Strongly disagree.




Demographic Variables
Several demographic variables required recoding:

Race/ethnicity (RaceEthn5) was converted to a factor and releveled so that Non-Hispanic White is the reference category in the regression model.
Education (EducA) was converted to an ordered factor with four levels: Less than High School, High School Graduate, Some College, and College Graduate or More. Less than High School serves as the reference category in the regression model.
Income required special handling. The original imputed income variable (IncomeRanges_IMP) had nine detailed income categories. To improve interpretability, these were collapsed into four broader categories: Less than $35,000, $35,000 to $74,999, $75,000 to $99,999, and $100,000 or more. The imputed version of the income variable was used rather than the original because it has only 0.2% missing data compared to 12.4% for the unimputed version. The NCI used a weighted sequential hot deck imputation method to fill in missing income values.
Age group (AgeGrpB) was converted to an ordered factor with five levels: 18–34, 35–49, 50–64, 65–74, and 75+. The 18–34 age group serves as the reference category.
Sex (BirthSex) was recoded to retain only Female and Male responses. The small number of respondents (n = 44) who responded “Don’t know” were set to missing because this group was too small for reliable estimation. Female is the reference category in the regression model.
Cancer history (EverHadCancer) and health insurance (HealthInsurance2) were each recoded as binary factors (No/Yes), with No as the reference category in both cases.



Frustration Variables
Four variables measuring the experience of seeking cancer information were recoded using the recode_hints() helper function to set invalid responses to missing. These variables: frustration_effort, frustration_quality, frustration_hard, and frustration_upset — were only asked of respondents who reported seeking cancer information (approximately 50% of the sample), so they are used only in the follow-up descriptive analysis restricted to information seekers.


Code
# Helper function
recode_hints <- function(x) {
  x <- as.character(x)
  ifelse(grepl("Missing|error|Error|Unreadable|Inapplicable", x), NA, x)
}

# Clean data
df <- df_raw |>
  mutate(
    seek_cancer_info = case_when(
      recode_hints(SeekCancerInfo) == "Yes" ~ 1L,
      recode_hints(SeekCancerInfo) == "No"  ~ 0L
    ),
    misleading_info = factor(
      recode_hints(MisleadingHealthInfo),
      levels = c("None", "A little", "Some", "A lot",
                 "I do not use social media")
    ),
    recs_conflict = factor(
      recode_hints(HealthRecsConflict),
      levels = c("Never", "Rarely", "Often", "Very Often")
    ),
    trust_doctor = factor(
      recode_hints(CancerTrustDoctor),
      levels = c("Not at all", "A little", "Some", "A lot")
    ),
    fatalism = factor(
      recode_hints(PreventNotPossible),
      levels = c("Strongly agree", "Somewhat agree",
                 "Somewhat disagree", "Strongly disagree")
    ),
    race = factor(recode_hints(RaceEthn5)),
    race = relevel(race, ref = "Non-Hispanic White"),
    education = factor(
      recode_hints(EducA),
      levels = c("Less than High School", "High School Graduate",
                 "Some College", "College Graduate or More")
    ),
    income = case_when(
      recode_hints(IncomeRanges_IMP) %in%
        c("$0 to $9,999", "$10,000 to $14,999", "$15,000 to $19,999",
          "$20,000 to $34,999") ~ "Less than $35,000",
      recode_hints(IncomeRanges_IMP) %in%
        c("$35,000 to $49,999", "$50,000 to $74,999") ~ "$35,000 to $74,999",
      recode_hints(IncomeRanges_IMP) == "$75,000 to $99,999" ~ "$75,000 to $99,999",
      recode_hints(IncomeRanges_IMP) %in%
        c("$100,000 to $199,999", "$200,000 or more") ~ "$100,000 or more"
    ),
    income = factor(income,
      levels = c("Less than $35,000", "$35,000 to $74,999",
                 "$75,000 to $99,999", "$100,000 or more")),
    age_group = factor(
      recode_hints(AgeGrpB),
      levels = c("18-34", "35-49", "50-64", "65-74", "75+")
    ),
    sex = case_when(
      recode_hints(BirthSex) == "Female" ~ "Female",
      recode_hints(BirthSex) == "Male"   ~ "Male"
    ),
    sex = factor(sex, levels = c("Female", "Male")),
    cancer_history = case_when(
      recode_hints(EverHadCancer) == "Yes" ~ "Yes",
      recode_hints(EverHadCancer) == "No"  ~ "No"
    ),
    cancer_history = factor(cancer_history, levels = c("No", "Yes")),
    insurance = case_when(
      recode_hints(HealthInsurance2) == "Yes" ~ "Yes",
      recode_hints(HealthInsurance2) == "No"  ~ "No"
    ),
    insurance = factor(insurance, levels = c("No", "Yes")),
    frustration_effort  = recode_hints(CancerLotOfEffort),
    frustration_quality = recode_hints(CancerConcernedQuality),
    frustration_hard    = recode_hints(CancerTooHardUnderstand),
    frustration_upset   = recode_hints(CancerFrustrated)
  )

# Survey design
svy <- df |>
  as_survey_rep(
    weights    = PERSON_FINWT0,
    repweights = num_range("PERSON_FINWT", 1:50),
    type       = "JKn",
    scale      = 0.98,
    rscales    = rep(1, 50),
    mse        = TRUE
  )




Handling Missing Values
The negative values in the HINTS dataset represent different types of missing data such as “Not Ascertained”, “Multiple responses selected in error”, ” Not Applicable”, “Skipped due to survey logic”. All the negative values were recorded to NA before the analysis. The HINTS 7 survey uses a complex sampling design, so to ensure that the estimates of our findings are representative of U.S adults, sampling weights were applied using the final sample weight (PERSON_FINWT0). Standard errors were calculated using 50 jackknife replicate weights (PERSON_FINWT1–PERSON_FINWT50), with a degrees of freedom of 49.

Missingness summary
Overall, the amount of missing values for our key variables was low. The outcome variable (seek_cancer_info) had less than 1% missing data. The race/ethnicity variable had the highest proportion of missing values at 11.5% (n = 840), which may affect the estimates for racial subgroups.All the other variables had less than 10% missing values. The missing values were excluded from analyses on a variable-by-variable basis.


Code
tibble(
  Variable = c("seek_cancer_info", "misleading_info", "recs_conflict",
               "trust_doctor", "fatalism", "race", "education",
               "income", "age_group", "sex", "cancer_history", "insurance"),
  `N Missing` = c(
    sum(is.na(df$seek_cancer_info)),
    sum(is.na(df$misleading_info)),
    sum(is.na(df$recs_conflict)),
    sum(is.na(df$trust_doctor)),
    sum(is.na(df$fatalism)),
    sum(is.na(df$race)),
    sum(is.na(df$education)),
    sum(is.na(df$income)),
    sum(is.na(df$age_group)),
    sum(is.na(df$sex)),
    sum(is.na(df$cancer_history)),
    sum(is.na(df$insurance))
  )
) |>
  mutate(`% Missing` = round(`N Missing` / nrow(df) * 100, 1)) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Missing data summary for analysis variables") |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_fill(color = "#FCEBEB"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = `% Missing` > 10)
  )

Missingness summary
Overall, the amount of missing values for our key variables was low. The outcome variable (seek_cancer_info) had less than 1% missing data. The race/ethnicity variable had the highest proportion of missing values at 11.5% (n = 840), which may affect the estimates for racial subgroups.All the other variables had less than 10% missing values. The missing values were excluded from analyses on a variable-by-variable basis.

title: Who Seeks Cancer Information?
text:
Faith Okaalo


University of Michigan


BIOSTAT 620 — Introduction to Health Data Science


April 2026






Using data from the Health Information National Trends Survey (HINTS 7), a nationally representative survey of 7,278 U.S. adults conducted by the National Cancer Institute in 2024, this project examines what predicts cancer information seeking and whether exposure to misleading health information plays a role.


 View Report   About the Data

title: Who Seeks Cancer Information? Predictors in a Nationally Representative U.S. Sample.
text:
Cancer remains one of the leading causes of morbidity and mortality in the United States. It is the second leading cause of deaths in the country after heart disease (Siegel et al., 2024). Availability and accessibility of accurate and timely information is necessary for prevention, early detection and treatment of cancer (Budenz et al., 2022; Finney Rutten et al., 2019). The cancer survivor population has been steadily growing over the past years which highlights the need for reliable health information to support treatment decision-making and follow up care. However, the information environment has become more advanced and complex for many to navigate. A survey conducted in 2021 reported that more than half of U.S. adults received contradicting and misleading information on alternative cancer treatment options on various social media platforms (Loeb et al., 2024). Research evaluating available cancer treatment information on social media platforms has found a substantial amount of information that is available online to be incorrect and potentially harmful (Johnson et al., 2022). Most individuals who use social media platforms as their primary sources of information perceive that most of the information to be misleading or false and a huge proportion approximately two-thirds find it challenging to distinguish true and false health information (Stimpson & Ortega, 2023).
Whether exposure to this kind of information encourages or discourages individuals from seeking cancer information or better answers remains a topic to be explored. Disparities in cancer seeking information patterns have been documented and persist in the United States (Budenz et al., 2022; Finney Rutten et al., 2019). The persistent racial disparities in cancer outcomes in the United States are further exacerbated by unequal access to timely information, prevention and early detection (Siegel et al., 2024). It is therefore vital to understand who seeks cancer information and what challenges they face in order to design tailored and equitable health communication interventions.
The main aim of this study is to examine what predicts cancer information seeking. Our research question is: What demographic and attitudinal factors predict whether U.S. adults seek cancer information, and is exposure to misleading or conflicting health information associated with cancer information seeking?

title: About the Data
text:

title: Who Seeks Cancer Information? Predictors in a Nationally Representative U.S. Sample.
text:
Methods

Data Source
This study used data from the Health Information National Trends Survey, Cycle 7 (HINTS 7), which is a nationally representative, cross-sectional survey of U.S. adults aged 18. The survey was conducted by the National Cancer Institute (National Cancer Institute, 2024). Data were collected between March and September 2024 using both mail and web-based surveys.The final sample consisted of 7,278 respondents. HINTS 7 is representative and uses a complex sampling design with with oversampling of minority populations to ensure adequate representation across the demographic groups.


Measures
The outcome variable of interest for this project was cancer information seeking, which was measured by a single item asking whether the respondent had sought information about cancer in the past year (yes/no). We had two key predictors which were information environment variables: exposure to misleading health information on social media (five categories: none, a little, some, a lot, and does not use social media) and frequency of encountering conflicting health recommendations (never, rarely, often, very often). Attitudinal predictors included our analysis were: trust in doctors for cancer information (not at all, a little, some, a lot) and cancer fatalism, measured by agreement with the statement that cancer prevention is not possible (strongly agree to strongly disagree). Demographic covariates included race/ethnicity, education, household income, age group, birth sex, personal cancer history, and health insurance status. Full details on variable coding are available on the About the Data page.


Statistical Analysis
Our analyses accounted for the complex sampling design of HINTS 7. Survey-weighted proportions were calculated to describe the prevalence of cancer information seeking across demographic groups. A survey-weighted logistic regression model was then fit to identify independent predictors of cancer information seeking. The results as reported as adjusted odds ratios with with 95% confidence intervals. All analyses were conducted in R.

title: Who Seeks Cancer Information? Predictors in a Nationally Representative U.S. Sample.
text:
Results

Prevalence of Cancer Information Seeking
Overall, approximately half 51% of U.S. adults reported seeking cancer information in the past year. Table 1 shows the weighted prevalence of cancer information seeking across demographic and attitudinal groups. Cancer information seeking increased with education level among adults with less than high school education to college graduates. There were also racial disparities with Non-Hispanic Whites having the highest information seeking rates compared to Hispanic and Non-Hispanic Black and Non-Hispanic Asian adults.








Figure 1: Weighted prevalence of information seeking by groups.




Figure 1. Weighted prevalence of cancer information seeking by demographic group




Predictors of Cancer Information Seeking
Table 2 presents adjusted odds ratios from the survey-weighted logistic regression model. Figure 2 displays these results visually as a forest plot, with blue points indicating statistically significant predictors (p < 0.05).
The strongest predictor of cancer information seeking was a personal history of cancer. Adults who had a cancer diagnosis had over four times the odds of seeking cancer information compared to those without (OR = 4.02, 95% CI: 2.96–5.45, p < 0.001). Frequently encountering conflicting health recommendations was also strongly associated with seeking cancer information.Compared to adults who never encountered conflicting recommendations, those who encountered them rarely had 3.78 times the odds of seeking information (OR = 3.78, 95% CI: 2.09–6.84, p < 0.001), those who encountered them often had 4.82 times the odds (OR = 4.82, 95% CI: 2.67–8.68, p < 0.001), and those who encountered them very often had 5.59 times the odds (OR = 5.59, 95% CI: 2.90–10.80, p < 0.001).
Higher education was associated with greater seeking. College graduates had approximately 2.79 times the odds of seeking cancer information compared to those with less than a high school education (OR = 2.79, 95% CI: 1.48–5.25, p = 0.002). Higher income was also associated with information seeking. Adults earning $75,000–$99,999 (OR = 1.48, 95% CI: 1.05–2.09, p = 0.027) and $100,000 or more (OR = 1.44, 95% CI: 1.07–1.94, p = 0.017) had significantly higher odds than those earning less than $35,000. Adults aged 35–49 had higher odds of seeking cancer information than those aged 18–34 (OR = 1.39, 95% CI: 1.03–1.88, p = 0.032). Compared to females, males had significantly lower odds of seeking information (OR = 0.76, 95% CI: 0.62–0.93, p = 0.007). Non-Hispanic Black adults had significantly lower odds of seeking compared to Non-Hispanic White adults (OR = 0.72, 95% CI: 0.54–0.95, p = 0.021), though differences for other racial and ethnic groups were not statistically significant after adjustment.
Notably, exposure to misleading health information on social media was positively associated with seeking cancer information. Those exposed to “a lot” of misleading information had higher odds of seeking than those exposed to none. However this association was not statistically significant. Trust in doctors for cancer information was also not significantly associated with seeking after adjusting for other factors.






Table 2. Survey-weighted logistic regression predicting cancer information seeking, HINTS 7 (2024)

Abbreviations: CI = Confidence Interval, OR = Odds Ratio










A forest-plot with adjusted odds ratios for predictors of information seeking




Figure 2. Adjusted odds ratios for predictors of cancer information seeking, HINTS 7 (2024)




Among Seekers: Was the Information Easy to Find?
As shown in figure 3, 59.1% of information seekers expressed concern about the quality of the information they found, making it the most commonly reported difficulty. Additionally, 41.8% found the information too hard to understand, 41.0% reported that it took a lot of effort to find cancer information, and 38.1% felt frustrated during their search. These findings suggest that even among adults who actively seek cancer information, the experience is often difficult and the quality of available information is a major concern.




Figure 3. Experiences among adults who sought cancer information, HINTS 7 (2024)




The Misinformation Paradox
One of the interesting finding from this analysis was the relationship between exposure to misleading health information on social media and cancer information seeking. Although one might expect misleading information to discourage engagement, higher exposure to misinformation was instead associated with greater cancer information seeking. Figure 4 illustrates this pattern. There was a clear increase in cancer information seeking with greater exposure to misleading health information, rising from 30.1% among those with no exposure to 58.4% among those with high exposure. Adults who did not use social media had an intermediate seeking rate of 37.7%. While this association was not statistically significant after adjusting for other factors in the regression model.




Figure 4. Cancer information seeking by level of misleading health information exposure, HINTS 7 (2024)

title: Who Seeks Cancer Information? Predictors in a Nationally Representative U.S. Sample.
text:
Conclusion and Summary
This study used data from the 2024 Health Information National Trends Survey (HINTS 7) to examine what predicts cancer information seeking among U.S. adults. Overall, approximately half of U.S. adults (51.1%) reported seeking cancer information in the past year, though this varied substantially across demographic and attitudinal groups.
The strongest predictor of cancer information seeking was a personal history of cancer, with survivors having over four times the odds of seeking information. This underscores the critical role of health information access in cancer survivorship and ongoing care. Encountering conflicting health recommendations was also strongly associated with information seeking, suggesting that people actively search for information when they are uncertain.
Education and income disparities in cancer information seeking were evident even after adjusting for other factors in the model. Adults with less education and lower incomes were significantly less likely to seek cancer information, which might be reflective of structural barriers in health information access. One of the most intriguing findings was a “misinformation paradox,” where adults exposed to higher levels of misleading health information on social media were more likely to seek cancer information. This pattern suggests that exposure to misinformation may act as a motivator rather than a barrier, potentially prompting individuals to verify the information they encounter.

Limitations
This study has several limitations.

HINTS 7 is a cross-sectional survey which makes it difficult to establish causal relationships.
All measures were self-reported and subject to recall and social desirability bias.
The race/ethnicity variable had the highest amount of missing values (11.5%), which may affect the precision of estimates for racial subgroups.
The survey captures whether cancer information was sought but does not include details on the quality or source of information found.



Future Directions
Future research could explore the misinformation paradox to understand whether early misinformation exposure leads to sustained information seeking over time. There is a need for interventions targeting low-education and low-income adults to help reduce disparities in cancer information access.

title: Who Seeks Cancer Information?
text:
About This Project
This website presents the final project for BIOSTAT 620. The analysis uses data from the Health Information National Trends Survey Cycle 7 (HINTS 7), a nationally representative survey of 7,278 U.S. adults conducted by the National Cancer Institute in 2024.
The primary research question guiding this analysis was to examine what demographic and attitudinal factors predict cancer information seeking among U.S. adults, and whether exposure to misleading or conflicting health information is associated with seeking behavior.

title: Who Seeks Cancer Information?
text:
Key Findings

Overall, 51.1% of U.S. adults sought cancer information in the past year
A personal history of cancer was the strongest predictor (OR = 4.02, p < 0.001)
Adults who frequently encountered conflicting health recommendations were over five times more likely to seek information
A striking misinformation paradox was observed — those exposed to more misleading health information were more likely to seek cancer information, not less
Education and income disparities in cancer information seeking persisted even after adjustment

title: Who Seeks Cancer Information?
text:

Report — Full analysis including introduction, methods, results, and conclusion
About the Data — Data source, variable descriptions, cleaning steps, and exploratory analysis


Data source: National Cancer Institute. Health Information National Trends Survey 7 (HINTS 7), 2024. Available at hints.cancer.gov
